publications.title: Genetic and structural basis for selection of a ubiquitous T cell receptor deployed in Epstein-Barr virus infection.
publications.authors: Miles JJ, Bulek AM, Cole DK, Gostick E, Schauenburg AJ, Dolton G, Venturi V, Davenport MP, Tan MP, Burrows SR, Wooldridge L, Price DA, Rizkallah PJ, Sewell AK.
publications.executive_summary: The study determined the crystal structure of AS01, a public TCR—one shared identically across many individuals—in complex with the immunodominant BMLF1 peptide from Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) and HLA-A*0201. The AS01 TCR contacts the peptide through a diagonal docking mode engaging two complementarity-determining region loops, and the receptor is encoded largely by germline gene segments that are statistically biased toward public TCR generation. This finding explains AS01's extraordinary prevalence in the human population: the responsible TCRα and TCRβ sequences are easily generated by V(D)J recombination, and the recognition of a ubiquitous pathogen appears to be "hard-wired" into the germline TCR repertoire, suggesting that the adaptive TCR system incorporates innate-like pattern recognition features. The work illuminates how both genetic predisposition and antigen-driven selection conspire to produce public TCRs, with implications for understanding population-level responses to persistent viral infection and informing rational TCR engineering strategies.
